title: Sauce Making
content:
For double-strength consommé: Add 3-4 ounces ground lean beef + egg white/shell per quart; plus uncooked fowl carcasses; tomato skins if beef stock.

Reducing: Evaporate to concentrate flavor. Double consommé = half original volume. Only for sauces without egg. Stir cream/flour-based sauces often to prevent scorching.

WHITE SAUCES

Thin: For cream soups
Medium: For creamed/scalloped foods
Thick: For soufflés
Very Thick: For croquettes

Basic proportions: Fat and flour equal parts (roux); add milk gradually. Season with salt, white pepper. For richer sauce: use part cream.

OTHER SAUCES

Brown Sauces: Based on brown stock; similar preparation to white.

Tartare: For fish, seafood, asparagus, cabbage, cauliflower, meats. Acid base.

Sweet Sauces: For desserts. Hard sauce for steamed puddings, gingerbread, mince pie. Fruit sauces for cottage pudding, cake, bread pudding, steamed puddings.

Court Bouillon: Seasoned cooking liquid for fish or vegetables. May be discarded after use or become fumet. Acidulated water reinforced with vegetables; or hot marinade with oil.

Fumet: Concentrated fish stock. Bones, trimmings, heads (gills removed) simmered with aromatics 15 minutes maximum (longer = bitter). Strain; use in soups, sauces, aspics.
